topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The stack of papers was an avalanche. Leo slumped onto the kitchen counter, the cheap laminate cool beneath his forearms. He hadn't slept properly in three days, the residue of late-night study sessions clinging to his eyelids like grit. As he sifted through the chaotic pile—geometry proofs, historical essays, forgotten poetry analyses—a strange sense of disorientation washed over him. His mother, usually meticulous about tidiness, had clearly amassed this entire repository of academic output. A faint, almost sad smile touched his lips. It was a bizarre comfort, realizing the sheer volume of effort she must have expended just to keep track of everything. He picked up a crumpled worksheet, the ink bleeding slightly under his thumb, and let his head fall back against the cabinet, the tension in his shoulders finally beginning to ease, replaced by a profound, heavy stillness.

[story 2]

"Seriously, Mom?" Sarah mumbled, pushing a stray strand of hair from her eyes. The fluorescent lights of the hallway seemed too bright, and the noise of the passing students felt amplified, grating against her nerves. She was running on fumes; every movement felt like lifting lead. Yet, when she found the box tucked deep in the attic, labeled simply "School Stuff," the sheer accumulation of forgotten homework hit her. It wasn't just papers; it was a testament to relentless, unseen labor. A slow, shaky breath escaped her lungs. She didn't move immediately, just stared at the evidence of years of parental sacrifice. A wave of exhaustion, deep and pervasive, settled over her limbs, making her want to simply dissolve into the dusty air.

[story 3]

Mark dragged himself across the floor of his bedroom, the effort feeling monumental. His vision swam slightly; the world seemed muffled and distant. He was supposed to be preparing for a presentation, but the words refused to coalesce. He found the box under his bed, the cardboard slightly warped with age. Inside, the sheer quantity of completed assignments—from kindergarten reports to final thesis drafts—was staggering. He didn't bother opening them all; instead, he just leaned against the wall, letting his gaze drift over the evidence. A low groan escaped him, a sound of pure depletion. The weight of responsibility, suddenly externalized in this tangible pile of paper, pressed down on him, making his muscles feel heavy and unresponsive.
